{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": null,
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "The plaintiffs alleged that Dr. Fishback negligently left a small piece of gauze or a few threads in the abdominal wound during the original appendectomy, and that this foreign body caused a large postoperative abscess on the surgical scar, resulting in additional surgery, pain, and damages.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "Plaintiffs relied on the patient’s testimony and his father’s testimony that Dr. Fishback had told them a piece of gauze or threads had been left in the wound; they asserted that such material would not be absorbed and would precipitate an abscess.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense asserted that Dr. Fishback found no foreign substance when he reopened the wound to drain the abscess and denied making any admission; the defense also argued that the plaintiffs offered no expert testimony to show that leaving gauze is a breach of the standard of care.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The appellate court held that it is common knowledge that leaving any gauze or foreign non‑absorbable material in a patient is not approved surgical practice, and that the question of whether the defendant actually left such material and caused the abscess should be decided by the jury, not by a directed verdict.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendant left foreign gauze/thread in wound causing abscess\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Patient and father testified that Dr. Fishback told them a piece of gauze or threads was left in the wound.\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant left foreign gauze/thread in wound causing abscess\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Doctor testified he found no foreign substance when he reopened the wound.\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant left foreign gauze/thread in wound causing abscess\",\"side\":\"court\",\"support_type\":\"other\",\"support_summary\":\"Court stated that leaving gauze is universally recognized as not approved practice and that the issue is for the jury.\",\"strength\":\"strong\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"The theory of the plaintiffs was that a foreign body had been negligently left in the wound at the time of the first operation and had caused the abscess.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Plaintiffs did not produce any direct testimony that a foreign substance was found in the course of the second operation, but relied upon Ronald Young’s own testimony and the testimony of his father, both reciting statements to that effect allegedly made to them individually by Dr. Fishback.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The doctor denied making any admissions, and said he found no foreign substance when he reopened the wound.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Everybody knows, without being told by an expert, that it is not approved surgical practice to leave in a patient’s body a small bit of gauze or a few threads therefrom, or any other foreign nonabsorbable substance, no matter how small.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "Opinion provides no operative description, no expert testimony, and no detailed medical record excerpts; extraction limited to procedural and testimonial content."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "During the February 20, 2007 operation, Dr. Smith removed tissue that pathology could not identify as an appendix, indicating a failed appendectomy. In August 2007, Dr. Rowell performed a second operation in which he removed the ileum, cecum and a retrocecal appendix that pathology described as chronically inflamed with perforation.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Wright alleges that Dr. Smith negligently performed a failed appendectomy by removing tissue that was not the appendix, thereby requiring her to undergo a second, successful appendectomy by another surgeon. She seeks recovery for the medical expenses of both surgeries, arguing that the defendant’s negligence caused the need for the second operation and the associated hospitalizations and treatments.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "Wright presented to the emergency department on February 20, 2007 with acute, possibly ruptured appendicitis and was admitted for surgery. Dr. Smith’s pathology report could not identify an appendix in the removed tissue. She later returned on August 13, 2007 with chronic appendicitis; Dr. Rowell removed the ileum, cecum and a perforated appendix, confirmed by pathology. Medical bills from both hospitalizations and surgeries are documented, with portions paid by Medicaid.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense contends that Wright’s appendicitis was not caused by any negligence of Dr. Smith and that the plaintiff must elect to recover damages for either the first or the second surgery, not both. They argue that post‑surgical treatments (venous duplex, pulmonary consult, UTIs, headaches) are unrelated to any alleged negligence and that expert testimony is required to link any damages to the defendant’s conduct.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The magistrate judge held that the plaintiff bears the burden of showing, through expert testimony, a causal link between Dr. Smith’s alleged negligence and her damages. The court found no negligence causing the need for a second appendectomy and warned against a windfall if both surgeries were compensated. It applied Virginia law on medical‑necessity, authenticity of bills, and the collateral source rule.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendant performed a failed appendectomy that did not remove the appendix\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Pathology could not identify an appendix in tissue removed by Dr. Smith\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Plaintiff should recover damages for both the initial and subsequent surgeries\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"legal_precedent\",\"support_summary\":\"Court held allowing recovery for both surgeries would create a windfall\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Medical expenses for post‑surgical treatments are unrelated to defendant’s negligence\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Treatments included duplex study, pulmonary consult, UTI, headaches not linked to surgery\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Plaintiff must show expert testimony that hospitalization was medically necessary and caused by defendant’s negligence\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Court required expert proof of causation and necessity for hospital bills\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Medicaid write‑offs should not reduce plaintiff’s recoverable damages\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"legal_precedent\",\"support_summary\":\"Court predicted Virginia Supreme Court would allow full amount of charges despite Medicaid write‑offs\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"Dr. Smith attempted an appendectomy on Wright... the pathologist was not able to identify an ‘actual appendix’ in the tissue removed.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Rowell surgically removed her ileum, cecum and appendix. The pathology report... showed chronic appendicitis with perforation.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The defendants argue that Wright cannot recover for both the surgery performed by Dr. Smith and the surgery performed by Dr. Rowell.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The plaintiff bears the burden of establishing... that there is a causal relationship between the violation and the alleged harm.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Wright must establish through expert testimony that her hospitalization and treatment at BRMC... were causally related to the defendants’ negligence.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "No expert witnesses were identified in the opinion; the analysis focuses on procedural and legal rulings rather than substantive expert testimony."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "After being transferred to the Puerto Rico Medical Center, plaintiff underwent an appendectomy on January 16, 1992. During that operation the surgeon nicked the right ureter, leading to urine leakage and fluid accumulation. Subsequent attempts to drain the urine failed, and a later surgery repaired the ureter. Ultimately the damaged right kidney was removed because of the ureteral injury.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Plaintiffs alleged that Hospital Metropolitano violated EMTALA by failing to provide an adequate medical screening and by transferring the uninsured patient without first stabilizing her condition. They further claimed that the hospital’s negligent care—misdiagnosing her condition and administering medications that masked her symptoms—caused a delay that led to a botched appendectomy at the receiving hospital, resulting in ureter injury and loss of her right kidney, for which they seek damages.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows the patient presented with abdominal pain, vomiting and nausea; vital signs were monitored, and a battery of labs, urinalysis and x‑rays were performed. Dr. Ferrer initially diagnosed gastroenteritis, then ruled out acute appendicitis, and the patient was transferred while stable. After the appendectomy at PRMC, imaging revealed a right ureter laceration and subsequent kidney removal.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "Defendants contended that the patient received a comprehensive screening—including labs, imaging and IV fluids—and was stable at transfer, satisfying EMTALA’s requirements. They argued that any injury resulted from the surgical error at PRMC, not from any omission or misstep by Hospital Metropolitano or Dr. Ferrer, and that the medications given could not have masked symptoms or caused harm.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Arturo Silvagnoli‑Collazo (expert witness for plaintiffs) testified that the initial diagnosis of gastroenteritis was incorrect and that medications administered might have masked abdominal pain, potentially delaying definitive care. He also linked the ureter injury to the earlier management, though he admitted no record showed the pain was actually masked.",
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Calimano (defendant’s expert) testified that the emergency department provided an excellent history, physical, labs and imaging, correctly ruled out acute appendicitis, administered appropriate fluids and analgesics, and that the patient was fully stabilized before transfer, meeting both EMTALA and the standard of care.",
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court found undisputed evidence that the patient received an adequate screening and was stable at transfer, citing both parties’ experts. It concluded there was no causal link between Hospital Metropolitano’s care and the ureter injury, which occurred during the appendectomy at PRMC, and therefore granted summary judgment for the defendants.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Hospital failed to provide adequate medical screening under EMTALA\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Patient received labs, x‑rays and IV fluids but plaintiff argues screening was insufficient\",\"strength\":\"weak\"},{\"claim\":\"Hospital transferred patient without stabilizing her condition\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff alleges she was still in pain at transfer\",\"strength\":\"weak\"},{\"claim\":\"Hospital’s misdiagnosis and medication masked symptoms leading to delayed surgery\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Dr. Silvagnoli‑Collazo testified medications could mask pain\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Hospital breached standard of care by inadequate screening and treatment\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Dr. Calimano testified screening was excellent and patient was stabilized\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"No causal link between Hospital’s actions and ureter injury\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Defendant expert stated injury occurred during PRMC surgery\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"No EMTALA violation because patient was stable\",\"side\":\"court\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Record shows vital signs stable and no deterioration during transfer\",\"strength\":\"strong\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"Plaintiff was taken to Hospital Metropolitano ... suffering from abdominal pain, vomiting, and nausea.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Ferrer ordered a battery of tests ... and prescribed some medications designed to relieve some of the pain.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The Court finds that plaintiff was afforded an adequate medical screening while she was at HM.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Calimano ... stated that this patient received excellent care at Hospital Metropolitano Emergency Room.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Expert_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Silvagnoli‑Collazo admitted ... the only thing he would have done differently ... was performing a rectal examination.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Expert_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "The opinion focuses on EMTALA and malpractice standards; no detailed intra‑operative findings beyond ureter laceration are provided. No mention of patient comorbidities."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": "Patient was found to have H. pylori infection indicating gastric ulcer disease and was diagnosed with gastritis during the course of care.",
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "CT scan on July 23, 2008 revealed a large pelvic abscess deemed secondary to perforated appendicitis. Dr. Brahan described a 11 cm pelvic abscess likely from a three‑week‑old ruptured appendix. The abscess was percutaneously drained, and an interval appendectomy was eventually performed on December 2, 2008.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Plaintiff alleges that prison medical staff deliberately ignored his severe abdominal pain and failed to obtain an appendectomy within the thirty‑day window recommended by his surgeon, constituting deliberate indifference under the Eighth Amendment. He contends the delay caused unnecessary suffering and worsened his condition, for which he seeks damages.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows repeated sick‑call requests for abdominal pain, vomiting, and fever; a CT scan demonstrating a pelvic abscess from perforated appendicitis; laboratory evidence of leukocytosis; and the eventual surgical drainage and appendectomy performed months later.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "Defense argues that the inmate received multiple evaluations, antibiotics, and referrals; that the surgeon scheduled a follow‑up and indicated the appendectomy could be delayed six to eight weeks; and that the prison physician lacked authority to schedule off‑site surgery, which was controlled by the MDOC’s Office of Medical Compliance.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The magistrate found no genuine issue of material fact that Dr. Woodall was deliberately indifferent; the medical records showed appropriate treatment, referrals, and that the delay was consistent with the surgeon’s plan for an interval appendectomy. Accordingly, the court granted summary judgment for the defendants.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendant failed to obtain timely appendectomy\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"CT showed perforated appendicitis and a large abscess; plaintiff did not receive surgery until Dec. 2, 2008\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant was deliberately indifferent to serious medical needs\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Multiple evaluations, antibiotics, and referrals were provided; surgeon advised interval appendectomy in 6‑8 weeks\",\"strength\":\"strong\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"A CT scan of Plaintiff’s abdomen and pelvis was performed, revealing a large abscess in Plaintiff’s pelvis, which was determined to likely be ‘perforated appendicitis.’\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Brahan’s impression was that Plaintiff had a ‘11 cm pelvic abscess, probably secondary to appendicitis of about 3 weeks duration.’\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Woodall treated Plaintiff numerous times for his gastrointestinal issues and prescribed various medications at different times.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The court finds that Plaintiff has failed to establish that Dr. Woodall was deliberately indifferent to his serious medical needs.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "No expert testimony was presented; the opinion focuses on procedural summary judgment and constitutional analysis rather than detailed malpractice standards."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "The plaintiff alleges that during the 1964 appendectomy Dr. Bolinger negligently left a surgical drain inside his body, which was not discovered until 1977 when it required removal, causing pain and suffering.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "The plaintiff, Delbert Baughman, claims that Dr. Bolinger breached the standard of care by leaving a surgical drain in his abdomen during the 1964 appendectomy. He asserts that the undiscovered foreign object caused injury that required a second operation in 1977, resulting in pain, suffering, and related damages. His wife, Linda Baughman, asserts a loss‑of‑consortium claim, alleging that her husband's incapacitation deprived her of companionship, services, and affection, for which she seeks damages.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The complaint states that a surgical drain was left in the plaintiff’s body during the 1964 appendectomy and remained undiscovered until 1977, when it was surgically removed, causing pain and suffering. No additional clinical records, imaging, or expert findings are detailed in the opinion.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense argues that the statute of limitations for the loss‑of‑consortium claim began to run at the time of the original 1964 surgery, contending that the alleged injury to the spouse’s rights should be deemed to have accrued then, not when the foreign object was discovered.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court applied Ohio law on foreign‑object cases and the discovery exception, concluding that the plaintiff’s rights were not injured until the foreign object was discovered in 1977. Accordingly, the loss‑of‑consortium claim accrued at that time, within the four‑year limitations period.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Loss of consortium accrued when foreign object was discovered\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff alleges the surgical drain left in 1964 was not discovered until 1977, at which point he required removal and suffered pain\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Statute of limitations began at time of original surgery\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"precedent\",\"support_summary\":\"Defense relies on Amer v. Akron City Hospital to argue the limitations period started in 1964\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"Defendant moved to dismiss the claim of Linda Baughman on the ground that it violates the applicable statute of limitations.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Delbert claims that during the 1964 surgery defendant negligently left a surgical drain in plaintiff’s body. Delbert further claims that this drain was not discovered until 1977 at which time it had to be removed surgically, causing him pain and suffering.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The Court holds that under these facts plaintiff Linda Baughman’s cause of action accrued for purposes of R.C. 2305.09(D) at the time her spouse discovered or reasonably should have discovered the alleged foreign object left within his body.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "Opinion contains no expert testimony or detailed medical records beyond the allegation of a retained surgical drain; therefore many fields are null."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "The veteran underwent an appendectomy at the Veterans Administration Hospital in Togus, Maine on June 7, 1947. No prior abdominal surgery was reported. Years later, on March 31, 1954, two metallic needle fragments were found between the diaphragm and liver, prompting exploration that revealed a subdiaphragmatic abscess.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "The plaintiff alleges that federal hospital staff negligently left needle fragments in his body during the 1947 appendectomy, constituting a wrongful act that caused chronic pain, repeated hospitalizations, and a subdiaphragmatic abscess. He contends the government’s failure to discover and remove the fragments, and alleged concealment, caused damages for pain, medical expenses, and lost earnings. The claim seeks recovery beyond the two‑year statutory period, arguing the cause of action did not accrue until the fragments were discovered in 1954.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows the veteran experienced right‑side pain beginning in 1948, with multiple hospitalizations from 1951‑1953 for pleurisy, duodenal ulcer, liver abscess, and other diagnoses. Imaging (X‑rays and fluoroscopy) eventually displayed metal fragments and diaphragm elevation. On February 22, 1954 he returned to Togus, and on March 31, 1954 needle fragments were identified and a subdiaphragmatic abscess was surgically explored.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The United States argued that the statute of limitations barred recovery for any negligence occurring before the claim accrued, which they held to be June 7, 1947, when the foreign objects were introduced. They maintained that subsequent treatment did not toll the limitations period and that the plaintiff’s amendment seeking recovery for continuing negligence was untimely.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court held that a claim under the Federal Tort Claims Act accrues when a private person similarly situated would become suable, which occurred on the date the needle fragments were left in the body (June 7, 1947). It found no legal basis for tolling the two‑year limitations period based on the plaintiff’s ignorance of the injury, and affirmed the district court’s finding of negligence for failing to discover the fragments by March 12, 1954.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendant left metal fragments in plaintiff’s body during appendectomy\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Appendectomy on June 7, 1947 and later discovery of needle fragments on March 31, 1954\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant failed to discover needle fragments earlier\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"District court found negligence in failure to discover fragments by March 12, 1954\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Statute of limitations bars recovery for negligence before March 31, 1954\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"legal_precedent\",\"support_summary\":\"Court held claim accrued in 1947 and was not tolled by later treatment\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Continuing negligence should toll statute of limitations\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"other\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff argued claim did not accrue until fragments were discovered in 1954\",\"strength\":\"disputed\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"Exercising his rights as an honorably discharged veteran of World War II, Tessier entered the Veterans’ Administration Hospital in Togus, Maine, for an appendectomy performed there on June 7, 1947.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"On March 31, 1954, the presence of two metallic needle fragments lying between appellant’s diaphragm and his liver was discovered, and an exploration thus prompted revealed a subdiaphragmatic abscess.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The district court found negligence in the failure of the Togus hospital personnel to discover the needle fragments by March 12, 1954, and thus find the abscess 19 days earlier than they did.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"In the course of this operation, the defendant, through its agents, servants or employees, used medical instruments and equipment in and about the body of the Plaintiff, whereupon, as a result of the negligence, wrongful act or omission of the Defendant… did cause, permit or place a piece or pieces of metal… in the Plaintiff’s body.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The statute provides that a ‘claim accrues’ when a private person similarly situated would become suable under the law of the state.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "No expert testimony is discussed in the opinion; the analysis focuses on statutory interpretation and factual findings. No explicit comorbid conditions were identified."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "Defendant Shah performed an appendectomy after a CAT scan and X‑rays indicated appendicitis. Post‑operative report stated the surgery was successful. However, ten days later Shah told the plaintiff he \"thought he removed plaintiff’s appendix, but it was tissue\" and the pathology report described the removed specimen as mature adipose tissue with acute and chronic inflammation and necrosis, with no definite appendix identified.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Plaintiff alleges that defendants were deliberately indifferent to his serious medical needs by misdiagnosing him with appendicitis, performing an erroneous surgery that failed to remove his appendix, misreporting the operation as successful, and providing no pain medication after discharge. He contends these actions caused continued abdominal pain, vomiting, and a need for a second surgery, violating his Eighth Amendment rights and seeking damages under 42 U.S.C. § 1983.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "Plaintiff reported severe abdominal pain beginning in January 2022, underwent CAT scan and X‑rays, and was consented for an appendectomy. After surgery he experienced ongoing vomiting and abdominal pain, received no analgesics, and was discharged. A surgical pathology report later showed no appendix was identified, only inflamed adipose tissue.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense argues that the plaintiff’s allegations amount only to negligence, not deliberate indifference, because there is no evidence that defendants knew of a substantial risk or purposefully ignored his pain. The post‑operative report noted the patient was in no acute distress and offered no complaints, and the plaintiff declined further treatment, undermining claims of intentional harm.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The magistrate applied the Eighth Amendment deliberate‑indifference standard, finding that plaintiff’s pleadings do not allege a purposeful act or knowledge of a substantial risk. The court concluded the allegations show at most negligence, which is insufficient for a constitutional claim, and therefore the complaint fails to state a cognizable claim.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendant performed surgery without removing appendix\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Surgical pathology report indicated no appendix was identified, only inflamed tissue.\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant misreported surgery as successful\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Shah later told plaintiff he thought he removed the appendix but it was tissue.\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant failed to provide pain medication after discharge\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff alleges nothing was given for pain and no recommendation to the prison.\",\"strength\":\"weak\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendants were deliberately indifferent to plaintiff’s serious medical needs\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"legal_standard\",\"support_summary\":\"Court held that allegations amount only to negligence, not purposeful indifference.\",\"strength\":\"strong\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"In January 2022, he began to experience severe abdominal pain and was taken to Banner Medical Center... defendant Shah, a surgeon, informed plaintiff that he had appendicitis and sought his consent to perform an appendectomy.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Ten days later, defendant Shah contacted plaintiff... he thought he removed plaintiff’s appendix, but it was tissue... the pathology report contained a final diagnosis that read: ‘mature adipose tissue with an apparent serosal surface with acute and chronic inflammation and necrosis. No definite appendix identified.’\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The deliberate indifference standard requires plaintiff to allege a purposeful act or failure to respond to a prisoner’s pain... Plaintiff’s allegations show that defendants evaluated him on intake and rendered a medical determination that an appendectomy was necessary... at most, negligence, which is insufficient to state a claim of deliberate indifference.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "The opinion contains no expert testimony or detailed medical records beyond the pathology report, limiting the depth of claim‑support analysis."
}

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": null,
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": null,
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "The plaintiff alleges that Dr. Sepulveda and Dr. Chudy were deliberately indifferent to his medical needs after learning that Dr. Ahmed refused to provide care, and that the defendants were responsible for an unreasonable delay in scheduling his appendectomy, causing him unnecessary pain. He seeks liability under 42 U.S.C. § 1983 for constitutional violations and damages for the alleged harm.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The plaintiff points to medical records showing Dr. Ahmed’s September 30, 2008 note that his abscess had resolved and that a follow‑up appointment and surgery were needed, a October 8, 2008 surgeon’s confirmation of clearance for surgery, and his own health‑service requests dated between October 8 and December 11, 2008 indicating continued pain and a request for an appendectomy.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense argues that there is no evidence they were aware of or responsible for scheduling the appendectomy; the plaintiff’s requests were routine and never reached the defendants, and the plaintiff’s belief that Dr. Ahmed caused the delay is speculative. The court finds the defendants’ summary judgment appropriate.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court examined the record and found a genuine dispute only regarding when Dr. Sepulveda and Dr. Chudy became aware of the plaintiff’s complaints, vacating summary judgment on deliberate indifference. Regarding the delay, the court concluded the plaintiff offered no evidence that any defendant controlled the surgery schedule, affirming summary judgment for the defendants.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Defendants were deliberately indifferent to plaintiff’s medical needs\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff points to Dr. Ahmed’s note that the abscess resolved and subsequent lack of action by Dr. Sepulveda and Dr. Chudy\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendants were responsible for delay in plaintiff’s appendectomy\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Plaintiff’s health‑service requests and statements of continued pain after October 2008\",\"strength\":\"weak\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendants were not responsible for scheduling delay\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Court found no record that defendants received or acted on plaintiff’s requests\",\"strength\":\"strong\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"The evidence, viewed in the light most favorable to plaintiff, reveals that on September 30, 2008, Dr. Ahmed confirmed that plaintiff’s abscess had resolved...\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Plaintiff alleged that he submitted five separate health services requests between October 8, 2008 and December 11, 2008...\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"There is no indication that any of plaintiff’s health care request services forms reached any defendant, nor that any defendant was aware after October 9, 2008, that plaintiff’s surgery had still not been scheduled.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The court concludes that plaintiff has pointed out new material facts that demonstrate a genuine dispute regarding when Dr. Sepulveda and Dr. Chudy became aware of plaintiff’s complaints, and whether they were deliberately indifferent...\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",
  "Extended_Extraction_Notes": "Opinion contains no operative details, comorbidities, or expert testimony; focus is on procedural/constitutional claims and summary‑judgment rulings."

{
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": "Plaintiff had a history of squamous cell carcinoma of the tonsil treated with chemotherapy and radiation (diagnosed 2019), mild renal insufficiency (GFR 57, creatinine 1.2), borderline chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube for nutrition.",

  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Plaintiff alleges Dr. Grijalva breached the standard of care by failing to continue intravenous antibiotics for 36‑37 hours after a ruptured appendix surgery, thereby allowing sepsis to worsen. These alleged breaches, he contends, caused short‑term sepsis, acute kidney injury, and long‑term neurologic deficits, for which he seeks damages and holds UPMC vicariously liable under respondeat superior.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows Plaintiff presented with severe abdominal pain, WBC 21,400, bands 48%, tachycardia (128 bpm), and respiratory rate >20, meeting UPMC’s sepsis protocol. Subsequent labs showed rising WBC (up to 58,300) and acute kidney injury. He later reported memory loss, headaches, hallucinations, and peripheral electric‑shock sensations.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "Defendants argue that the source of infection was removed surgically, that short‑course antibiotics are standard after adequate washout, and that Plaintiff’s elevated WBC reflects a leukemoid reaction rather than ongoing sepsis.   "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Arnold L. Lentnek, board‑certified internal medicine and infectious disease specialist, opined that Plaintiff was immunocompromised, had established intra‑abdominal sepsis at presentation, and that failure to administer continuous Zosyn for at least four doses allowed the infection to “flourish,” causing both short‑term and long‑term sequelae, including sepsis‑associated encephalopathy.",
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Steven D.   "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The magistrate found genuine issues of material fact regarding whether the delayed antibiotics constituted a deviation from the standard of care and whether they caused Plaintiff’s injuries. {
  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": "Mr. Drenner had a history of Crohn’s disease diagnosed around 2005 and a history of kidney stones prior to the December 2012 events.",
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "During the December 6, 2012 laparoscopic procedure Dr. Femi‑Pearse encountered a phlegmon and was unable to clearly identify the cecum or appendix. He excised a necrotic‑appearing structure, stapled it, and sent it to pathology, which later reported the specimen as bowel wall, not appendix. Two drains were placed. Post‑operative notes documented purulent drainage, later feculent material, and suspicion of a bowel leak. On December 14, 2012 Dr. Powell’s exploratory surgery revealed a quarter‑size perforation at the tip of the cecum (consistent with a missed or inadequately sealed appendix site) and a second perforation at the base of the terminal ileum, with diffuse purulent ascites and stool contamination.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "The plaintiff alleges that Dr. Femi‑Pearse breached the standard of care by proceeding with a laparoscopic appendectomy despite severe inflammation and failing to convert to an open procedure, resulting in an improperly stapled cecal tissue and a subsequent bowel leak. The leak caused peritonitis, sepsis, multiple additional surgeries, permanent abdominal disability, and extensive economic and non‑economic losses.",
  "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows Mr. Drenner presented with right‑sided abdominal pain; CT imaging demonstrated progressive inflammatory change in the pericecal region; the operative report admits uncertainty in identifying the appendix; pathology did not confirm appendix removal; post‑op progress notes recorded purulent then feculent drainage and a CT suggesting a leak; internal medicine consult noted sepsis and high risk of abdominal leak; subsequent surgery confirmed a cecal perforation and stool contamination.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "The defense, through Dr. Vaziri, contended that the laparoscopic approach was appropriate, that Dr. Femi‑Pearse safely identified and divided a structure resembling the appendix, and that conversion to an open operation was unnecessary and would have added pain and risk. The defense argued the complications were a result of the disease process rather than surgical error.",
  "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": "Dr. David Dreyfuss, board‑certified general surgeon, testified that Dr. Femi‑Pearse breached the standard of care by not converting to an open procedure and by stapling severely inflamed tissue, which a prudent surgeon would have avoided; he asserted that an ileocolectomy would have been the appropriate response and that the breach caused all subsequent injuries.",
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Khashayar Vaziri, board‑certified surgeon, testified that Dr. Femi‑Pearse’s laparoscopic surgery was within the standard of care, that the identified structure could be safely stapled, and that conversion was not required; he maintained that the procedure was performed safely and that any leak was a known risk of the underlying disease.",
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court concluded that Dr. Femi‑Pearse failed to convert to an open operation despite a phlegmon that obscured anatomy, that his uncertainty in identifying the appendix constituted a breach, and that this breach directly caused the cecal leak and all subsequent surgeries. The court relied on the operative report, pathology, intra‑operative photos, and the plaintiff’s expert testimony, finding the plaintiff’s evidence more credible than the defense’s.",
  "Claim_Support_Matrix_JSON": "[{\"claim\":\"Failure to convert to open surgery when anatomy was obscured\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Dr. Dreyfuss testified conversion was required under the circumstances\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Improper stapling of inflamed cecal tissue leading to leak\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"clinical_fact\",\"support_summary\":\"Post‑op purulent and feculent drainage, CT suggesting leak, and operative findings of cecal perforation\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Causation: leak caused subsequent surgeries and damages\",\"side\":\"plaintiff\",\"support_type\":\"medical_record\",\"support_summary\":\"Progress notes, CT scans, and Dr. Powell’s testimony linking stool leak to the initial surgery\",\"strength\":\"strong\"},{\"claim\":\"Defendant’s procedure was within standard of care\",\"side\":\"defense\",\"support_type\":\"expert_testimony\",\"support_summary\":\"Dr. Vaziri testified the laparoscopic approach was safe and conversion unnecessary\",\"strength\":\"moderate\"}]",
  "Evidence_Quotes_JSON": "[{\"quote\":\"Dr. Femi‑Pearse said, ‘I cut something out—I don’t know what, but we cut something out.’\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The Pathology Report states that the cut fragment may represent a portion of bowel wall, not an appendix.\",\"topic\":\"Operative_Findings_Detail\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Progress notes on Dec. 11 showed feculent, stool‑like matter and concern for a possible fecal fistula.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Dreyfuss testified that Dr. Femi‑Pearse should have converted to an open procedure and that stapling inflamed tissue was akin to stapling wet toilet paper.\",\"topic\":\"Plaintiff_Expert_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"Dr. Vaziri testified that the surgery was performed safely in a laparoscopic manner and conversion would have caused more pain.\",\"topic\":\"Defense_Expert_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null},{\"quote\":\"The court finds Dr. Femi‑Pearse should have converted from a laparoscopic procedure to an open procedure and his failure to do so is a breach of the standard of care.\",\"topic\":\"Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary\",\"location_hint\":null}]",

  "Comorbid_Diagnoses_Text": "Powers has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), diabetes, atrial fibrillation, and morbid obesity (weighed 258‑275 lbs during the period at issue).",
  "Operative_Findings_Detail": "Powers underwent an appendectomy at Madison Community Hospital on September 6, 2018. Post‑operative examination revealed a large incisional hernia at the right of the umbilicus that grew from a nontender bulge in October 2018 to a \"football‑sized\" mass by February 2019 and later to a \"basketball‑sized\" hernia. Physical notes described bowel palpated within the hernia sac, and imaging (ultrasound) showed no obstruction, strangulation, or incarceration, but the hernia’s size and pain persisted.",
  "Plaintiff_Claims_Expanded": "Powers alleges that Dr. Eddy, as the State Medical Director, deliberately ignored his serious medical need by repeatedly denying CT imaging and surgical follow‑up for the rapidly enlarging incisional hernia.   "Plaintiff_Medical_Support_Summary": "The record shows Powers’ hernia was noted as a large incisional bulge on October 1, 2018, grew to football‑size by February 2019, and caused daily severe pain, limited ambulation, and constipation. Ultrasound on January 3, 2019 reported findings at odds with physical exam, and a later CTA in September 2020 confirmed extensive loss of domain. Weight gain (258‑275 lb) and comorbid COPD and atrial fibrillation are documented.",
  "Defense_Medical_Rebuttal_Summary": "Defendant argues the hernia was reducible, not at imminent risk of strangulation, and that surgery posed excessive risk given Powers’ obesity, COPD, and atrial fibrillation.   "Plaintiff_Expert_Summary": null,
  "Defense_Expert_Summary": "Dr. Daniel Eiferman and Dr. David Lindsey (both hernia specialists) provided opinions that Powers’ weight and loss of abdominal domain made surgical repair high‑risk with only a 40‑50% chance of success, supporting the decision to deny surgery.",
  "Court_Medical_Reasoning_Summary": "The court evaluated the objective prong (herniated mass, pain, functional loss) and found genuine disputes over whether the treatment was grossly inadequate. It also examined the subjective prong, noting insufficient evidence that Dr. Eddy consciously disregarded a known substantial risk. Accordingly, the court concluded material factual issues remain and denied summary judgment.",
